details.text: Derivative: الْأَنفَالُ (Anfaal) is the plural of نَفَل (Nafal) which refers in origin to something that is “extra” that has been given or a “gift”. It is used to refer to the spoils of war.
Derivative: تَطْمَئِنَّ is from اِطْمَأَنَّ - يَطْمَئِنُّ – اِطْمِئْنَانًا which means to be calm and at ease i.e., to be in a state of peace and tranquillity.
Derivative: يُذْهِبَ comes from أَذْهَبَ – يُذْهِبُ – إِذْهَاب. ذَهَبَ means “to go” whilst أَذْهَبَ means “to make something to go away” i.e., to remove and get rid of it.

Derivative: يُغَشِّي: غَشِيَ – يَغْشَى – غَشَاوَة means “to cover” something. غَشَّى – يُغَشِّي – تَغْشِيَة is the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Emphasised and accentuated) form of غَشِيَ and means to completely “cover” or “envelop”.
Derivative: يَرْبِطَ is from رَبَطَ - يَرْبِطُ – رَبْط which means to “bind, tie and fasten”. In respect hearts it is used to mean “to fortify and strengthen” them.
Derivative: شَاقُّوا is from شَاقَّ – يُشَاقِقُ – شِقَاق / مُشَاقَّة which means “to oppose”. It implies an opposition borne out of taking a side based on animosity to the other side.

Derivative: زَحْفًا comes from زَحَفَ in origin depicts the movement of “crawling” and in respect to an army it means “to advance” or “march” slowly and steadily. زَحْف means “advance” or “march”. زَحْفًا implies in a state of “marching or advancing”.
Derivative: مُتَحَرِّفًا comes from تَحَرَّفَ which means to deviate and to turn in a different direction. Here it implies the meaning of to change one’s position i.e., tactically in battle.
Derivative: مُتَحَيِّزًا comes from تَحَيَّزَ which with إِلَى implies the meaning of joining to something or someone else. In battle it refers to joining with other forces or reinforcements.

Derivative: يُبْلِيَ is from أَبْلَى – يُبْلِي – إِبْلاء which means to “test” someone for the purpose of refining him. That is whilst بَلَى (Balaa) just means “to test” and اِبْتَلَى (Ibtalaa) means “to test with a difficult or trying test”.
Derivative: مُوهِنُ (Moohin) comes from أَوْهَنَ – يُوهِنُ – إِيهَان which means to weaken. مُوهِنُ (Moohin) refers to the one who is constantly weakening something.

Derivative: تَسْتَفْتِحُوا comes from اِسْتَفْتَحَ which can mean to seek help and victory and can also mean to seek a final decision or resolution to a matter. This is as الفَتْح (Al-Fat’h) can imply the meaning of “help and victory” and the meaning of “a decision or verdict”.
Derivative: وَجِلَتْ comes from وَجِلَ - يُوجَلَ – وَجَلًا which means to feel frightened or fearful. It describes an intense fear when your hair stands up on your skin and you tremble.
Derivative: إِنَّمَا (Innamaa) is an expression of "exclusivity" in Arabic. It establishes both emphasis (from إِنَّ) and exclusivity.
Derivative: يَحُولُ comes from حَالَ and with بَيْن (Bayna – Between) it means “to come between” two things or “to interject or put a barrier” between them.

Derivative: يَتَخَطَّفَ comes from تَخَطَّفَ – يَتَخَطَّفُ – تَخَطُّفًا which means “to seize and snatch away” like a bird of prey snatching its prey. Here snatching away can be understood to refer to kidnapping or abduction. It can also just portray the feeling of weakness and fear in the case where you can just “be snatched away” like a weak animal or insect can be snatched away by its predator.
Derivative: يَرْكُمَ comes from رَكَمَ – يَرْكُمُ – رَكْمًا which means to pile and heap up or to accumulate and amass.

Derivative: غَنِمْتُم is from غَتِمَ – يَغْنَمُ – غَنِيمَة which means to acquire غَنِيمَة (Ghaneemah) which refers to everything that is taken from the battlefield in terms of booty and spoils of war. It is also said to refer to the properties taken by force and in war.
Derivative: الْعُدْوَةِ (‘Udwah) refers to side or slope of an elevation like of a valley or hills.

- So that Allah decides or brings about (لِّيَقْضِيَ اللَّهُ) a matter (أَمْرًا) that was already (كَانَ) done (مَفْعُولًا)” - كَانَ مَفْعُولًا “Was already done” is in expression to mean that it was already ordained.
Derivative: تَنَازَعْتُمْ comes from تَنَازَعَ which literally implies “pulling or tugging amongst one another” and means “to contend, quarrel and dispute amongst each other”.

Derivative: تَفْشَلُوا comes from فَشِلَ - تَفْشَلُ - فَشْلًا which in contemporary Arabic is used to mean “to fail” but in classical Arabic it was used to mean “to lose heart, courage or motivation”.
- It literally means “Your wind (رِيحُكُمْ) goes (تَذْهَبَ)” and is similar to the English expression of “losing the wind beneath your sails”. رِيحُ (Reeh – “Wind”) implies strength and power.

Derivative: بَطَرًا comes from بَطِرَ which means to be full of oneself. بَطَر (Batar) is “Arrogance, conceit and cockiness”. بَطَرًا (Bataran) describes their condition (حَال - Haal) and means “in a state of conceit and arrogance”.
Derivative: نَكَصَ means “to withdraw, retreat and to turn or shrink away from something”. In origin it is used when someone turns away from doing something good or a good intention.

Derivative: دَأْبِ comes from دَأَبَ – يَدْأَبُ – دَأْب which means to persist and continue upon doing something. دَأْب (Da’b) refers to that which someone has persisted and continued upon i.e., a habit, custom or legacy.

Derivative: شَرِّدْ is the command form from شَرَّدَ – يُشَرِّدُ – تَشْرِيد which was used to describe frightening away camels when one was slaughtered in front of them. Here it refers to making an example by punishment that would frighten away and disperse others like them i.e., an exemplary punishment meted out to some from a criminal group that would cause the rest of the criminal group to be frightened and disperse.
- رِّبَاطِ الْخَيْلِ (Ribaat ul-Khail) refers to horses that are kept and reserved solely for warfare.

Derivative: جَنَحُوا comes from جَنَحَ – يَجْنَحُ – جُنُوح which means to be inclined or to lean towards something. جَنَاح (Janah) also refers to the wing of a bird and جَنَحَ can imply a meaning similar to the English expression of “lowering one’s wings”.
Derivative: حَرِّضِ from حَرَّضَ – يُحَرِّضُ – تَحْرِيض which means to rouse (enthusiasm), to incite and spur on.
Derivative: يُثْخِنَ is from أَثْخَنَ - يُثْخِنُ – إِثْخَان which means to exhaust and weaken. In battle it means to inflict severe losses on the enemy as a demonstration.

Derivative: الشَّوْكَةِ (Shawkah) is a thorn and is used due to its sharpness and strength to refer to might and power. Here it is referring to the meaning of being armed for war or having military might.
- To قَطْع (Qat’ – “Cut”) دَابِر (the Daabir – “Side or Root”) of something is an expression to eradicate it or cut it off from its roots.
Derivative: آوَوا is the plural of آوَى – يُؤْوِي – إِيوَاء and it means to provide refuge and shelter.

Derivative: أَوْلِيَاءُ (Awliyaa’) is the plural of وَلِي (Waliy) and implies someone who has a bond of protective friendship and loyalty. أَوْلِيَاءُ (Awliyaa’) implies protective and loyal friends or close allies.

Derivative: تَسْتَغِيثُونَ is from اِسْتَغَاثَ – يَسْتَغِيثُ – اِسْتِغَاثَةً. غَيْث (Ghaith) refers to “rain” that comes at the time of need and meets the need. اِسْتِغَاثَةً (Istighaathah) means to seek help at the desperate hour and time of urgent need in order to meet that need.

Derivative: مُرْدِفِينَ is the plural of مُرْدِف (Murdif) which comes from the verb أَرْدَفَ – يُرْدِفُ – إِرْدَاف. رَدَفَ means for something to follow and come immediately after another. مُرْدِف (Murdif) refers to that which has been made to follow immediately after another. The meaning of مُرْدِفِينَ here means in succession i.e., groups or rows of Angels one group following directly after the other.
Derivative: اِسْتَجَابَ – يَسْتَجِيبُ – اِسْتِجَابَةُ means to respond and answer i.e., a response that is accepted and answered.
